chapter_summary: Suparna (Garuda) carries the serpents, the sons of Kadru, swiftly through the sky to a lovely ocean-girt island resounding with birdsong and adorned with flowering groves, lotus-pools, clear lakes, and fragrant breezes that delight Gandharvas and Apsaras. Having sported there with joy, the serpents demand that the great-strong Suparna bear them on to yet another delightful, well-watered island, since as a flyer he sees many beautiful regions below. Suparna, reflecting, asks his mother Vinata why he must do the serpents' bidding. Vinata answers that she has been made a slave of her ignoble sister (Kadru) because the serpents, by deceit, established a false wager against her. Grieved by his mother's sorrow, the sky-goer asks the serpents what he must fetch, learn, or accomplish by valor to be released from their bondage. The serpents reply that he must forcibly bring the amrita (nectar of immortality); only then will he and his mother be freed from slavery.

relationships.rel: enslaved Vinata by a false wager

story.title: Suparna Learns the Price of His Mother's Freedom
story.arc: Garuda bears the serpents to a paradisal island; when they imperiously demand to be carried further, he questions his mother Vinata and learns she is enslaved to her sister Kadru through the serpents' deceitful wager. Resolved to free her, he asks the serpents their price, and they name it: the amrita, seized by force. The episode sets in motion Garuda's heroic quest for the nectar of immortality.
story.timeline_note: Part of the Astika Parva of the Adi Parva; follows the curse and the deceitful wager between Kadru and Vinata, and precedes Garuda's expedition to seize the amrita.
story.comic_potential: The contrast of the serpents lounging in paradise yet still petulantly ordering their winged porter onward to an even nicer resort — the ultimate entitled passengers bullying the world's strongest courier — has rich comic irony before the heroic turn.

teachings.teaching: True bondage is born not of fate but of deceit accepted: Vinata's slavery springs from a false wager fraudulently imposed, showing how a single act founded on untruth can bind one in servitude.
teachings.lens_note: In Sharma's Kriya framework, untruth (asatya) and the false agreements of the lower mind are precisely the bonds (granthi) that enslave consciousness; liberation begins when the seeker, like Suparna, asks clearly what binds him and resolves to undo it at the root.

teachings.teaching: Devoted action to free one held in bondage is the worthy aim: grieved by his mother's sorrow, Suparna does not lament but asks what feat of valor will win her release.
teachings.lens_note: Sharma reads such resolve as the awakened will turning grief into disciplined effort — the aspirant who, instead of mourning the soul's captivity, seeks the very means (here, the amrita) by which immortality and freedom are won.

teachings.teaching: The price of liberation is the nectar of immortality, seized by one's own valor: the serpents declare that only by bringing the amrita will Suparna be freed from slavery.
teachings.lens_note: For Sharma the amrita is the inner nectar (the deathless essence reached through Kriya), and the teaching that freedom comes only by attaining it mirrors his view that release from the bondage of mortality is won by direct, effortful realization, not by petition.
